So before I jump into the second part, there is a topic that I want to talk about that's about how to systematically connect the training process, the internal testing or the validation that you have as well as testing on the test set.
And this process, this systematic process is called k-full cross validation.
The idea behind k-full cross validation is that you're only having one internal test set.
You're only giving yourself one time chance to see some example of unseen data set.
But actually, what you can do is that you can, you grab a chunk of your data, you treat that as internal test set.
But there are multiple different chunks of 20%, for example, data that you can extract from your training data.

So why limit yourself to only one chunk? Specifically, the issue is going to be that if you're going to randomly select 20 samples and limit yourself to that exact 20% sample that you extracted, just an example, there is kind of a chance that you get lucky or you get unlucky in that only one shot that you have.

So for example, here, but I have is that randomly selecting 20%, again randomly selecting 20%, again randomly selecting 20%, do the training, tested on that specific kind of random set of validation data that we extracted, but you can see that the validation error is very different from one iteration to another iteration.
So we don't want to leave everything to luck, but we want to make sure that we're systematically letting each data point in our training set to at least once sit in the internal test set.
And that's why what we can do is systematically always keeping multiple different kinds of validation of spools in our training pipeline.
So these chunks that we're looking at, these are called folds, right?
So for example, what I've showed you previously was that, okay, take your training data, keep a percentage of that for training mini and keep a percentage for validation.
But I always showed that as only keeping the last 20% or last x% of data to be your validation set.
But in fact, what you can do is you can go ahead and grab the first chunk, the second chunk, the third chunk and the fourth chunk if your folds are going to be 20 percent size.
This will let each one of the data points in your entire data set to be able to sit in the validation set at least once.
And then nothing is going to be left to chance because there's always those different kinds of validations that you have, right?
And you're systematically checking the data set or the training pipeline that you have under multiple different combinations of training data points and also validation data points.
So that's called K-fold cross validation. But K-fold cross validation, we say that we're going to divide our entire data set into multiple folds.
And K stands for how many folds you have here.
So for example, if I'm going to say that my validation chunk is going to be 20% of my data, I will have five chunks of 20%.

Right? And then what I can do is I can say that what I'll do is I will perform this algorithm, this systematic algorithm that's called cross validation that every time one chunk is going to be validation, the residue chunks are going to be training and then I will move on to the other chunks one at a time and then I'll perform the model link in this case is going to be five times because I have five chunks each one of them should be my validation set at least or exactly once.
So this K-Full Cross Validation, if I want to systematically explain what's happening is that you divide your data, you randomly shuffle your data point, right?
Again, what you will do is after you randomize your data set, you will split it into K-folds, depending on what percentage of your data points are going to be in validations at every time.
So for example, if your validation fold is going to be 10%, you will have 10 folds.
Effectively, you're doing 10 fold cross validation. In my example, my fold percentage was 20%, so I'm having five fold cross validation.
So I'm dividing it into K number of faults. I will train the model on every fold, other than the validation fold.
I'll keep the validation fold out. What I'll do is I will compute the model error on the validation fold and I will record it.
Then what happens is that I'll repeat this thing for all of the K-folds that I have.
So I'll get basically multiple different models. and different models are going to be tested on different validation sets and they're going to lead to different errors.
And we're going to talk about why that's important and why that's actually helpful in our process.
We use the term cross validation error as the average of the error of all of these models.
